coredata.dc:title: Enhanced performance of lithium-ion battery cathodes using a composite conductive network of CNTs, GQDs, and GNRs embedded in Li₁.₂Mn₀.₅₄Ni₀.₁₃Co₀.₁₃O₂ (LMNCO)

coredata.dc:description: Lithium-rich Li-ion battery cathode materials are known for their high specific capacities and working potential. However, their practical application is limited by the significant decline in discharge potential and capacity during repeated cycling. In this study, we introduce a highly conductive electrode architecture, encapsulating within carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene nano-ribbons (GNRs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to mitigate rapid capacity fading and suppress voltage decay. Three Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 cathode materials were synthesized. The first sample, pristine Li-rich L1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, was synthesized using a nitrate co-precipitation method. The second cathode, GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, incorporates a mixture of carbon nanotubes and graphene nanoribbons. The third cathode, GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2), further integrates graphene quantum dots within the graphene nanoribbons and carbon nanotubes matrix. The inclusion of highly conductive GNRs-CNTs and GQDs effectively enhances the electrical conductivity of L1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, increasing the specific capacity from 246.95 to 316.94 mAhg−1. Additionally, GQDs prevent side reactions and surface phase transitions, thereby improving thermal stability.

head.ce:title: Enhanced performance of lithium-ion battery cathodes using a composite conductive network of CNTs, GQDs, and GNRs embedded in Li₁.₂Mn₀.₅₄Ni₀.₁₃Co₀.₁₃O₂ (LMNCO)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-rich Li-ion battery cathode materials are known for their high specific capacities and working potential. However, their practical application is limited by the significant decline in discharge potential and capacity during repeated cycling. In this study, we introduce a highly conductive electrode architecture, encapsulating within carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene nano-ribbons (GNRs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to mitigate rapid capacity fading and suppress voltage decay. Three Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 cathode materials were synthesized. The first sample, pristine Li-rich L1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, was synthesized using a nitrate co-precipitation method. The second cathode, GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, incorporates a mixture of carbon nanotubes and graphene nanoribbons. The third cathode, GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2), further integrates graphene quantum dots within the graphene nanoribbons and carbon nanotubes matrix. The inclusion of highly conductive GNRs-CNTs and GQDs effectively enhances the electrical conductivity of L1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, increasing the specific capacity from 246.95 to 316.94 mAhg−1. Additionally, GQDs prevent side reactions and surface phase transitions, thereby improving thermal stability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have emerged as critical and innovative energy storage solutions in recent years, finding wide applications in power tools, electric vehicles, and large-scale energy storage systems [1,2]. LIB-powered electric vehicles, in particular, are considered the future of sustainable transportation due to their efficiency and eco-friendly characteristics [2,3]. However, the driving range of current electric vehicles is limited by the technical constraints of LIBs, underscoring the urgent need to develop high-energy LIBs [4,5]. Enhancing the performance of the cathode, a pivotal component of LIBs, is especially crucial.

The cathode materials currently employed in commercial LIBs, such as LiCoO₂ (layered), LiFePO₄ (olivine), and LiMn₂O₄ (spinel), along with their derivatives, fall short of the increasing energy density demands due to their relatively low discharge capacities [6–8]. This highlights the necessity to explore cathode materials capable of achieving higher energy densities.

Lithium-rich manganese layered oxides (LLO) have attracted significant attention as promising high-energy cathode materials due to their high discharge capacities. LLO materials comprise two main phases: a rhombohedral LiMO₂ phase (where M represents Ni, Co, or Mn) and a monoclinic Li₂MnO₃ phase [9]. Differentiating these phases is challenging given their structurally similar layered configurations [10,11]. The typical composition of LLO is expressed as Li₁₊zMᵧMn₁₋ᵧ₋zO₂ (where 0.1 < z < 0.3) [12], though debates continue regarding the precise structure of these materials. Studies by Jarvis et al. [10] and Yu et al. [11] indicate that Li₂MnO₃ and LiMO₂ may form a solid solution. LLOs exhibit high discharge capacity over a voltage range of 2.0 to 4.8 V vs. Li+/Li. Notably, below 4.4 V, the capacity primarily arises from the LiMO₂ component. When the voltage exceeds 4.4 V, the extraction of Li + and O2− ions from Li₂MnO₃ creates an electrochemically active MnO₂ framework, but this process also leads to an irreversible capacity loss in the first cycle [12].

Lithium-rich manganese-based cathode materials, such as Li₁.₂Mn₀.₅₄Ni₀.₁₃Co₀.₁₃O₂ (LMNCO), are widely utilized for their cost-effectiveness, low toxicity, and high specific capacity, particularly in lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) for electric vehicles [13]. Despite these advantages, the commercialization of LMNCO faces significant challenges due to structural transformations and side reactions at the electrolyte interface, which negatively impact cycle performance, voltage stability, and rate capability [14]. Addressing these challenges has become critical, as issues like limited rate capability, rapid capacity fade, and low initial coulombic efficiency continue to hinder progress [15]. One approach to mitigate these drawbacks involves introducing a conductive surface layer to enhance electron and ion transport properties, which can improve rate capability [16,17]. Furthermore, capacity fade in LMNCO arises from factors such as phase transformations, phase separations, transition metal dissolution, metal migration, and detrimental side reactions at the electrode/electrolyte interface [18–21]. Surface modification has shown promise in stabilizing these interfaces, thereby enhancing electrochemical performance and durability [22]. To effectively preserve the structural integrity of LMNCO particles, this surface layer must also provide robust mechanical stability to prevent cracking and sustain the long-term effectiveness of surface modifications [23,24]. The composite of GQDs:GNRs-CNTs has not been previously reported, and past LMNCO syntheses have relied heavily on metal carbonate and acetate precursors, which present challenges in achieving optimal purity and phase stability.

Carbon-based materials like CNTs, RGO, and GQDs are widely employed to modify electrode materials due to their excellent electrical conductivity, structural stability, and accessibility of active edge sites. These conductive nanomaterials are non-toxic, offer high electron mobility, and are compatible with the LIB operating environment, making them invaluable in LIBs for enhancing electron transport and structural integrity [25–27]. To date, to the best of our knowledge, no research has reported the use of a composite of graphene nanoribbons and CNTs (GNRs-CNTs) synthesized via the unzipping of multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) to modify lithium-rich cathodes. Likewise, while GQDs have been used to enhance Li₁.₂Mn₀.₅₄Ni₀.₁₃Co₀.₁₃O₂, their synthesis has traditionally involved complex and costly methods [25].

In this study, LMNCO was synthesized via a nitrate co-precipitation method, utilizing the high solubility of nitrates in water and ethanol to achieve homogeneous atomic-level mixing. This uniformity is critical for synthesizing complex multi-component materials such as Li₁.₂Mn₀.₅₄Ni₀.₁₃Co₀.₁₃O₂, promoting phase purity and enhanced electrochemical performance. Additionally, nitrates decompose at lower temperatures, releasing nitrogen oxides rather than carbon-based residues (common with acetates or carbonates), thereby reducing contamination and improving crystallinity. Conductive additives were prepared by unzipping MWCNTs to form GNRs-CNT composites, while GQDs were synthesized through an innovative and straightforward solvothermal approach. Two modified cathode materials, GNRs-CNT@Li₁.₂Mn₀.₅₄Ni₀.₁₃Co₀.₁₃O₂ and GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li₁.₂Mn₀.₅₄Ni₀.₁₃Co₀.₁₃O₂, were then developed and systematically compared to pristine LMNCO to evaluate the influence of these carbon nanomaterial composites on electrochemical performance.

Li1.2 Mn0.54Ni0.13 Co1.3O2 was synthesized by mixing stoichiometric amounts of lithium nitrate monohydrates (Li(NO3).H2O, Sigma Aldrich, 99.9 %), nickel nitrates hexahydrates (Ni(NO3)2 .6H2O, Sigma Aldrich, 99.9 %), manganese nitrates tetrahydrates (Mn(NO3)2.4H2O, Sigma Aldrich, 99.9 %) and cobalt nitrates hexahydrates, (Co(NO3)2 .6H2O, Sigma Aldrich, 99.9 %) in 10 mL ethanol and then adding 0.108 mL polymethyl phenyl siloxane in the solution. The prepared solution was stirred for half an hour at 80 ̊C to obtain a pink color precipitate. The resulting mud-like pick precipitate was then heated at 80 ̊C under vacuum for 24 h till it turned dark pink. Following this step, the precipitate was further heated at 500 ̊C for 5 h, transforming it into a black color crude material, which was then ground to obtain a fine black powder of Li1.2 Mn0.54Ni0.13 Co1.3O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene nanoribbons and un-zipped CNTs
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene nanoribbons were synthesized through the exfoliation of 95 % pure multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Initially, 0.5 g of MWCNTs was dissolved in 20 mL of concentrated H2SO4 and continuously stirred for 48 h. Subsequently, 2.5 g of KMnO4 (Shanghai, 99.99 %) was gradually introduced into the ongoing reaction, maintaining a pH below 3. Subsequently, 5 mL of H2O2 was slowly introduced dropwise into the reaction mixture under an ice bath. The resulting solution was then heated and stirred at 65 °C for 2 h. Following the reaction, the mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm, and the precipitate was washed with a 5 % hydrochloric acid solution and deionized water until reaching pH 4. The resulting thick residual was subsequently dried at 900 °C in a vacuum furnace under an ammonia atmosphere [28].

GQDs were synthesized using a solvothermal method. A 10 mg/mL suspension of MWCNTs in NN dimethylformamide (DMF) was prepared under ultrasonic dispersion for 1 h. Subsequently, the suspension was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After the reaction, the GQDs/DMF was vacuum filtered, and the residual DMF was evaporated to produce solid GQDs. 0.25 g of the obtained GQDs were ultrasonically dispersed for 20 min in 50 mL ethanol [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GNRs-CNTs modified Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 materials
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the cathode electrode of conductive networks, 0.05 g GNRs-CNTs obtained exfoliation of MWCNTs was dispersed in 50 mL ethanol, and ultrasonicated for 1 h. Then 1 g Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 was added into suspensions slowly and magnetically stirred at 80 °C till ethanol evaporated then transferred to a vacuum oven to dry more at 80 °C to get GNRs-CNTs modified Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs-coated GNRs-CNTs modified Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 materials
ce:section.ce:para: 0.5 g of GNRs-CNTs modified Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 was slowly added in the GQDs dispersed 50 mL of ethanol. This mixture was stirred for 1 h using a magnetic stirrer. After 1 h stirring, the mixture was placed in a water bath, and stirring was continued at 80 °C for evaporation of ethanol. Then it was transferred to a vacuum oven to dry at 80 °C. Thus, the GQDs surface-coated GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 material was obtained [27]. (See Fig. 1 .)

Powder x-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to characterize the structure and phase of the prepared samples, utilizing Cu Kα radiation (Miniflex-300, Rigaku), in the 2θ range of 10° to 80° at a scanning rate of 1°/min with a step size of 0.02°. Subsequently, Rietveld refinement was applied to the XRD data for the precise determination of lattice parameters. To discern the ion valence states in the metal oxide, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using VGESCA-LABMK II was employed. The microstructural analysis was conducted using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, SU 8020, HITACHI). Further insights into the microstructure of S-NCM and H-NCM powders were obtained through Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) using a Hitachi H-7650 instrument. Raman Microscope by RENISHAW UK with Excitation laser 488 nm, Laser Exposure time10 s, grating 1800 I/mm, objective x50Land laser power100%. For the electrochemical performance of the cathodes, the electrolyte consisting of a 1 mol/L LiPF6 solution in an ethylene carbonate (EC) and dimethyl carbonate (DMC) mixture with a volume ratio of 11 was prepared. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed with a VersaSTAT 3 potentiostat Galvanostat between 2.0 and 4.8 V (vs. Li+/Li) at a scan rate of 0.1 mV/s. Galvanostatic charge and discharge (GCD) tests were conducted within a potential range of 2–4.8 V (vs. Li/Li+) at room temperature for all samples to provide insights into their electrochemical behavior. The CV test voltage range of 0.6–1.1 V is selected to investigate low-potential features related to redox reactions occurring at these voltages, such as Mn3+/Mn4+ and Ni2+/Ni4+ transitions. However, the high operating voltage region (2.0–4.8 V) is used for cycling and rate performance tests, where higher voltages are applied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural, morphological, elemental, and optical analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The structure information of all samples was characterized using powder X-ray diffraction using (Cu Kα, λ ~ 0.15418 nm) as shown in Fig. (2a). All diffraction peaks are prominent, indicating the good crystalline structure of all the materials present. All the samples are two-phase composite systems with rhombohedral LiM3+O2 (M = Ni, Co, and Mn) phases of layered rock-salt hexagonal structures with an R-3 m space group [30]. The sharp peak gradually turned to a lower angle attribute that there is a change in (300) d-spacing relative to the pristine material. The characteristic peaks appearing near 20–25° result in the formation of superlattice ordering of Li+ and transition metal ions (such as Ni2+, Co3+, and Mn4+), which correspond to cation ordering in the transition metal layer in the Li2MnO3 phase [31,32]. The two adjacent peaks of the (020) and (110) planes around 20.8° clearly indicate the existence of a monoclinic Li2MnO3 phase with a space group of C/2 m symmetric structure. Most of the XRD diffraction peaks of Li2MnO3 overlapped with that of LiMO2 [33]. The characteristic diffraction peak around 25° confirms the presence of graphene quantum dots, which is further analyzed in RAMAN analysis. The adjacent peaks (006)/(012) and (018)/(110) show obvious splitting, indicating that both GNRs-CNT@L1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 and GQD:sGNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 samples have a highly ordered crystal layered structure [32]. The diffraction peaks (300) and (101) have shifted toward a higher 2θ value which indicates the shrinkage in the cell volume [34]. The shift to the increasing c parameter may be due to the result of the surface of LMNCO forming a solid solution [35]. The lattice parameter generally indicates the distance between transition metal atoms in the transition metal layer. The lattice parameter c represents the interlayer spacing parameter.

All as-prepared Li-rich layered oxides exhibit c/a ratio values that are sufficiently high to form well-developed hexagonal layered structures. The increasing c/a ratio in the doped samples (see Table 1 ) suggests a lower degree of transition-metal ion mixing in the lithium layer [36]. The intensity ratio I(300)/I(104) is commonly employed to assess the degree of cation mixing in the layered structure of Li-rich cathode materials, and it remains unchanged after coating the lithium layer with transition-metal ions [33]. A higher intensity ratio (R) value indicates better structural order and reduced cation mixing between Li+ and Ni2+, whose ionic radii are comparable. For all samples, the R value exceeds 1.2, although it gradually decreases as the coating amount increases, implying that more transition-metal ions migrate into the Li layer with increased coating. The I(300)/I(104) ratio >1.2 serves as a reliable indicator of a more ordered cationic structure and improved cation ordering [30]. In GNRs-CNT@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 and GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 samples phase structure of LMNCO has not been modified by the addition of GNRs-CNTs and GQDs in LMNCO. It can be concluded from the results that in the GNRs-CNT@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 and GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 materials the surface of the LMNCO are homogeneously coated [27]. No significant lattice parameter differences were recognized; also, no additional reflections corresponding to carbon are observed due to its amorphous structure, as it does not affect the crystal structure of LMNCO.

The Raman spectrum, obtained across the spectral range of 900–3300 cm−1, provides essential insights into the structural characteristics of the synthesized GQDs. The spectrum displays two prominent peaks corresponding to the D and G bands, with the D band appearing in the 1315–1335 cm−1 range and the G band at 1595 cm−1, consistent with previous studies [37]. The D band, associated with sp3-hybridized carbon, reflects the disorder introduced by incomplete carbon‑carbon bonding at the edges of the GQD's disordered crystal structure. Although no additional Raman peaks were detected, the intensity of the G band exceeded that of the D band. This D band generates new electronic states due to structural disorder, affecting the A1g vibrational mode linked to in-plane breathing motions of carbon atoms—a characteristic of graphene and other 2D materials. Conversely, the distinct G band, representing the E2g vibrational mode, corresponds to stretching in the sp2-hybridized carbon network [38]. The presence of second-order spectral features, such as combinations of 2D, D + G, and 2G bands, further confirms the material's graphitic nature. The measured ID/IG intensity ratio of 0.85 indicates a balanced degree of crystallinity, where values exceeding 1 suggest defect presence. To further explore the unique optical properties of GQDs, photoluminescence (PL) analysis was performed. The PL spectrum shows emission peaks at 580 nm and 675 nm, indicating that the GQDs primarily emit in the red region of the visible spectrum. Complementary UV–Vis spectroscopy (Fig. 2d) reveals a single peak at 320 nm for the optimally prepared GQDs solution, with absorption intensity decreasing as wavelength increases. The calculated bandgap of 3.8 eV, along with a noticeable blue shift in peak position, underscores the quantum confinement effects within the GQDs [39]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted to elucidate the elemental composition and chemical states of the transition metals (Li, Mn, Ni, Co, O) and carbon in the GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li₁.₂Mn₀.₅₄Ni₀.₁₃Co₀.₁₃O₂ composite. The complete XPS spectra confirmed the presence of these elements across all samples, with no extraneous elements detected apart from carbon, attributed to the reference sample [40]. In the C1s spectrum, a prominent peak at 286.7 eV was linked to C–O–C/C–OH groups, while a secondary peak at 293.2 eV corresponded to R–O–(CO)–O–R. (See Fig. 3 ). The distinct carbon satellite structure in GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li₁.₂Mn₀.₅₄Ni₀.₁₃Co₀.₁₃O₂ is attributed to the graphitic carbon surface contribution from the GQDs [27].

A closer examination of the transition metal oxidation states via Ni 2p spectra revealed two main peaks at Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 with binding energies at 855.4 eV and 875.2 eV, respectively, in the GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li₁.₂Mn₀.₅₄Ni₀.₁₃Co₀.₁₃O₂ sample. Additionally, two Ni satellite peaks associated with Ni oxides (e.g., LiNiO₂) were identified. Nickel and cobalt exhibited similar satellite peaks due to shared electronic splitting characteristics. Notably, the Ni signal intensity increased significantly with the addition of GNRs-CNTs and GQDs, enhancing electronic interaction with the LMNCO. The Co 2p peaks were detected at Co 2p3/2 (778.9 eV) and Co 2p1/2 (796.3 eV), indicating Co3+ presence. A slight shift to higher binding energy in the Co 2p peaks implies reduced electron density around the Li₁.₂Mn₀.₅₄Ni₀.₁₃Co₀.₁₃O₂ framework. The absence of satellite peaks and reduced Co 2p intensity in the GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li₁.₂Mn₀.₅₄Ni₀.₁₃Co₀.₁₃O₂ sample likely reflects the variation in Co content across samples. The Mn 2p3/2 peaks further reveal oxidation states consistent with Mn3+ and Mn4+, marked by excitation energies at 640.4 eV and 653.0 eV, respectively. These findings suggest that the unique structural and compositional modifications achieved through GQDs, GNRs, and CNTs integration confer superior electronic properties, promoting stability and performance in LMNCO-based lithium-ion batteries.

The Mn 2p binding energy gradually shifted downward after the doping of GQDs:GNRs-CNTs in LMNCO, indicating a transition from Mn4+ to Mn3+. The presence of Mn3+ suggested the formation of spinel lithium manganese oxide LiMn2O4 on the material's surface. The binding energy of O 1 s at 532.8 eV was considered chemisorbed oxygen, representing lattice oxygen in LMNCO. The three distinct spectra of O 1 s in Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 indicated the lattice structure of O2 oxygen, surface adsorbed oxygen, and carbonate species resulting from CO2 reactions in the environment. The migration of O1s to higher binding energy correlated with the stability of surface oxygen, influenced by the binding energy of Mn4+ in Li-rich Mn-based materials [35]. The study highlights the significant effects of each transition metal on surface stability and oxidation, influencing the electrochemical properties of the material [41]. The extraction of Li+ ions from the LiMO2 component with a connected oxidation state of transition metal ions was confirmed through electrochemical studies.

The morphological characteristics of the composite materials, namely Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, and GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, were investigated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Microscopic images were captured at magnifications of approximately 5 μm (Fig. 4 a, b, c (i)) and 500 nm (Fig. 4 a, b, c (ii)). The magnified SEM image of Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 shown in Fig. a (ii) revealed a polyhedral structure with a smooth surface, indicating uniform growth [42]. Following the coating of GNRs-CNTs, the SEM image (Fig. 4 b (ii)) depicted the presence of dispersed small particles on the surface of the Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 sample. The GNRs were observed to be randomly distributed and connected with CNTs, forming a reinforced conductive network [25]. In the GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 sample, all particles exhibited interconnected characteristics, with the appearance of GQDs coating onto GNRs-CNTs@LMNCO materials being subtly pronounced. Elemental composition analysis through energy-dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDS) shown in Fig. a, b, c (iv) confirmed the presence of all materials after doping, with the addition of carbon in both samples compared to the pristine state. The particle size distributions of all three samples (see Fig. a, b, c (iii)) displayed a uniform range from 100 to 500 nm. The average particle diameters of the prepared samples were measured at 90 nm, 100 nm, and 140 nm, respectively. It is evident that smaller particle sizes in the material correspond to faster de-intercalation of lithium ions [43].

Fig. 5 (a) displays the TEM image of GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 showed the successful growth of GQDs onto GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 [27]. The image illustrates numerous GQDs, each with a size <10 nm, some of which are highlighted with red dotted circles. The surface microstructure affirms the existence of a composite conductive network comprising GQDs, GNRs, and CNTs within the active material. The surface coating of GQDs is distinctly observable. The 3D porous structure of primary particles, coupled with their small size, facilitates electrolyte penetration into the electrode, creating a short channel for swift Li+ ion diffusion. This design enhances the speed of Li+ ion de-intercalation and intercalation [44]. STM image, shown in Fig. 5 (a), shows the perfect structure of graphene for graphene quantum dots.

The EDX elemental mapping of the prepared sample is presented in Fig. 5b, illustrating a uniform distribution of all elements within the sample. Li, however, could not be detected in the EDX elemental mapping due to its exceptionally low energy [43].

To investigate the electrochemical redox reactions, the initial two cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves for all three samples were recorded shown in Fig. 6 (a, b, c), revealing typical lithium-rich layered cathode material behavior. The test voltage ranged between 0.6 and 1.1 V, with a sweep rate of either 0.1 or 1 mVs−1. The first cycle exhibited a single major oxidation and reduction peak, while the second cycle showed two anodic and cathodic peaks. These anodic peaks corresponded to the two charging platforms during the charging process [43].

In the initial oxidation peaks around 0.65 and 0.75 V for GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, the redox reaction of transition metal Ni4+/2+ and Co4+/3+ in the layered structure occurred. The second oxidation peaks around 0.91 and 0.92 V were associated with the conversion of Li2MnO3 into Li2O and MnO2 by releasing oxygen. This process led to a highly irreversible activation of the Li2MnO3 component and the extraction of Li+ from the host material [43,45].

Compared to the pristine Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, the increased intensity of the anodic peak suggested O2− release [46]. The initial reduction peaks at 0.96 and 0.84 V, and the second reduction peaks at 0.74 and 0.64 V, corresponded to the reduction of Ni4+/2+, Mn4+/3+, and Co4+/3+ to higher oxidation states, respectively [44]. In the second cycle, the peak strength of Ni and Co ions increased, with enhanced intensity of the oxidation peaks and a shift in position to higher voltage, and reduction peaks to lower potential. This behavior was attributed to polarization and the effective conductive network of GQDs and GNRs-CNTs in the active material [25]. The observed phenomena indicated that the coating contributed to improved electrochemical reversibility, and the suppressed voltage fading in coated samples positively influenced the preservation of the layered structure [35].

The galvanostatic charge/discharge tests (Fig. 6 (d, e)) were conducted within the voltage window of 0–5.0 V (vs. Li/Li+), with a discharge capacity at a current rate of 1C (250 mAh g−1). All samples displayed typical charge/discharge characteristics, featuring a prominent plateau between 3.5 and 4.5 V in the first cycle. The specific capacity for the first cycle was determined to be 246.58 mAh g−1 with a coulombic efficiency of 70.84 % for LMNCO, 248.59 mAh g−1 with a coulombic efficiency of 71.06 % for GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, and 261.78 mAh g−1 with coulombic efficiency of 75.05 % for GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2. The specific capacity in the slope region was nearly identical among the three samples, with longer plateau regions. In the second cycle, the specific capacities were 246.95, 278.11, and 316.94 mAh g−1 with coulombic efficiencies of 71.21 %, 80.59 % and 90.56 % for LMNCO, GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, and GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, respectively.

The charging curves exhibited an upward slope below 4.5 V, corresponding to the oxidation of transition metal components Ni2+ to Ni4+ and Co3+ to Co4+ in LiMO2, while the voltage plateau around 4.5 V indicated the oxidation of the Li2MnO3 component. During the charge profile, all charging curves displayed a smooth slope region below 3.0 V. The specific discharge capacity increased in the second cycle for all samples, with charging curves showing a smooth slope below 4.0 V, representing oxidation transformations (Ni2+/Ni4+ and Co3+/Co4+) and removal of Li2O from Li2MnO3, respectively. The extended plateau disappeared during the second cycle, indicating the irreversibility of the removal of Li2O from Li2MnO3. The specific discharge capacity of GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 was larger than the other two samples in both cycles, attributed to higher electrochemical impedance and conductivity due to the coating layer, reduced polarization, and increased specific capacity. The voltage of GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 faded with cycling, primarily ascribed to structural evolution.

Fig. 7a illustrates the rate capability of Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, and GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 electrodes. As the current rate increases from 0.2C to 5C, the discharge specific capacities of the three electrodes decrease from 239.4, 254.4 and 282.3 mAh g−1 to 88.8, 113.4 and 125.9 mAh g−1, respectively, corresponding to the capacity retention rates of 37.09, 44.58, and 44.60 %, respectively. The GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, and GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 electrodes exhibit significantly better rate performance compared to the Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 electrode. Upon returning to a current rate of 0.2C, all electrodes display lower capacities compared to their initial values at 0.2C, which can be attributed to capacity loss during cycling from 0.2C to 1C.

Following the rate performance test, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were conducted at 50 % state of charge (SOC), and the results are presented in Fig. 7b. Each Nyquist plot shows two arcs and a slope. The sheet resistance (Rs) is represented by the Z' axis intercept at high frequency, while the arc at high frequency corresponds to the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) resistance (RSEI), and the semi-circle at intermediate frequency reflects the charge transfer resistance (Rct) between interfaces. The low-frequency slope is associated with Warburg diffusion impedance (ZW). To obtain kinetic parameters, equivalent element fitting was performed by fitting a model of equivalent circuit on the electrodes. The equivalent circuit model is shown in the inset of Fig. 7b, with kinetic parameters obtained presented in Table 2 . It can be observed that the Rs, RSEI, and Rct values for the GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, and GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 materials are notably smaller than those of the pristine Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2. Specifically, the Rs values for the GQDs-modified electrodes are reduced, indicating enhanced conductivity.

The RSEI values of GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, and GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 are also smaller compared to Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, suggesting that surface coating of carbon nano-materials effectively reduces direct contact between the electrode and electrolyte, limiting SEI film growth. Additionally, the Rct values of the carbon nanomaterials modified electrodes are significantly reduced due to improved electron transport and interfacial charge transfer. Both GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, and GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 electrodes exhibit lower impedance, explaining their superior rate capabilities.

Fig. 7 (c) presents the cycling stability of Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 and GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 electrodes at a 1C rate. After 150 cycles, the discharge specific capacities of these electrodes decrease from 170.3, 180.1 and 191.9 mAh g−1 to 107.7, 132.8 and 166.9 mAh g−1, respectively, corresponding to capacity retention rates of 63.24 %, 73.74 % and 87 %. These results demonstrate that the combinatory conductive network of CNTs, GQDs, and GNRs significantly enhances cycling stability, with the GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 electrode exhibiting both the highest initial specific capacity and the best overall cycle stability.

Fig. 8 (a, b, c) illustrates the midpoint discharge voltage of Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, and GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 respectively for the cycles (1st, 10th, 50th, and 100th) at a current rate of 1C. All three electrodes decayed from 2.22, 2.27, and 2.30 V to 1.96, 2.01, and 2.03 V after 100th cycle respectively. The discharge profile of Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 shows fast voltage fading as the cycle number increases. The coated samples show slightly less DMP than the pristine sample for 100 cycles due to the combined conductive network of carbon-based nanomaterials [47]. Besides, the voltage fading is faster in pristine samples compared to coated samples indicating the benefit of holding the voltage stability [48,49].

To determine the ideal sintering temperature, thermogravimetric analysis (TG) of the precursor was conducted (see Fig. 9 ). In the temperature range of 30–800 °C, three distinct heat decompositions were observed, indicating the influence of the crystal structure on electrochemical performance.

The TG curves of the composite materials, including Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 and GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, exhibited substantial weight losses across various temperature ranges. The breakdown of lactic acid and acetate primarily contributed to the second mass loss stage between 250 and 500 °C. A minor weight loss step between 500 and 600 °C constituted the fourth stage. The TGA curve remained almost horizontal in the temperature range between 600 and 800 °C, indicating the formation of a solid structure. Based on these observations, 600 °C was selected as the optimal temperature for secondary calcination to ensure the complete decomposition of nitrates.

In summary, three composite-based cathode materials, including Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2, and GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 were synthesized and characterized. Among these, the GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 displayed the most favorable electrochemical performance, achieving a specific discharge capacity of 316.94 mAh g−1 at 0.2C. When the current rate was increased from 0.2C to 5C, the capacity retention remained at 44.6 %. After 150 cycles at 1C, the discharge capacity declined from 199.9 to 166.9 mAh g−1, reflecting a retention rate of 87 %. The improved thermal stability of GQDs:GNRs-CNTs@Li1.2Mn0.54Ni0.13Co0.13O2 was confirmed by TGA analysis. The CNTs establish a robust and integrated conductive network by connecting the LMNCO particles, while the GNRs provide extensive contact, and the GQDs surface coating significantly enhances the interfacial charge transfer of the LMNCO material. Electrochemical analyses indicate that the presence of GQDs mitigates side reactions between the active material and electrolyte and thereby reduces both potential and capacity decay during cycling, resulting in a marked improvement in electrochemical performance.
